current_fact.fact_text: On this day in 1598, Juan de Oñate began the conquest and colonization of Santa Fe de Nuevo México. His expedition forged one of the northernmost outposts of New Spain, leaving a legacy tangled with missionary work, Indigenous resistance, and contested memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

current_fact.match_reason: A story of colonial expansion into Indigenous lands is mirrored by an episode centered on Tuareg voices, whose music carries histories of territory, resistance, and survival under outside powers.

queue.fact_text: In 1305, Roger de Flor, leader of the mercenary Catalan Company, was assassinated in Adrianople, sparking a wave of revenge raids across Thrace. His death turned a hired army into an uncontrolled, roaming force that carved its own bloody path through the Byzantine world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: Mercenary armies running wild and political rap‑metal uprisings share a mood of volatility, aggression, and systemic breakdown.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1943, the British submarine HMS Seraph surfaced off the coast of Spain to release a corpse dressed as a courier, carrying fake Allied invasion plans. This macabre deception, codenamed Operation Mincemeat, successfully misled Nazi intelligence about where the Allies would land in the Mediterranean.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A secretive submarine mission built on misdirection pairs with an open‑ended, mood‑driven mix that invites listeners into hidden currents and unexpected narrative twists.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1945, deep in the Führerbunker beneath Berlin, Adolf Hitler and Eva Braun took their own lives less than 40 hours after marrying. Above ground, Soviet soldiers raised the Victory Banner over the Reichstag, symbolizing the collapse of Nazi Germany and a turning point in world history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: The end of a devastating regime and the symbolic banner over Berlin align with an episode about storms passing, transitions, and choosing hope after dark chapters.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1961, the Soviet Union commissioned K‑19, its first nuclear submarine equipped with ballistic missiles. Nicknamed 'Hiroshima' by its own crew for its accident‑prone design, the vessel became infamous after a catastrophic reactor leak in 1961 that irradiated sailors and nearly caused a nuclear disaster at sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A nuclear‑powered submarine and its dangerous reactor issues connect thematically to an episode pulsing with electricity, energy, and the edge between power and risk.

queue.fact_text: In 1963, the Bristol Bus Boycott began after the city’s bus company refused to employ Black or Asian crews. Inspired by the U.S. civil rights movement, local activists used economic pressure and public protest to challenge everyday racism in Britain and ultimately forced the company to back down.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A boycott against racist hiring practices resonates with an episode about breaking boxes, confronting prejudice, and affirming identity through conscious lyrics.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1973, during the unraveling of the Watergate scandal, U.S. President Richard Nixon fired White House Counsel John Dean while top aides H. R. Haldeman and John Ehrlichman resigned. The dramatic 'Saturday Night Massacre' atmosphere signaled that the cover‑up was collapsing from within.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A scandal that forced a nation to re‑examine trust, power, and truth connects with an episode about unlearning, questioning assumptions, and thinking again about what we believe.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1975, Communist forces captured Saigon, prompting frantic helicopter evacuations from rooftops and the iconic image of people scaling the U.S. embassy wall. The 'Fall of Saigon' marked the formal end of the Vietnam War and the beginning of a painful reckoning for millions on both sides.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: After one of the most intense and traumatic wars of the 20th century, the idea of a 'slow planet' invites a counter‑imaginary of de‑escalation, healing, and gentler rhythms.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1980, six armed men stormed the Iranian Embassy in London, taking 26 hostages and triggering a tense six‑day siege. The crisis ended in a dramatic live‑broadcast assault by Britain’s SAS, turning counter‑terrorism into global television spectacle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: Both the embassy siege and the episode are anchored in London’s streets; one shows the city under siege, the other offers a musical love letter to its enduring, creative energy.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1982, the Bijon Setu massacre unfolded in Kolkata when a mob lynched 16 monks and a nun from the Ananda Marga order. The attack, fueled by rumor and suspicion, remains a haunting example of how misinformation and fear can erupt into sudden, collective violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: The horror of mob violence born from closed minds is countered by an episode about movement, perspective, and the kind of travel that softens fear into understanding.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1999, neo‑Nazi David Copeland detonated a nail bomb at the Admiral Duncan gay pub in London’s Soho, killing three and injuring 79. It was the last in a series of attacks targeting Black, Asian, and LGBTQ+ communities, and it galvanized public solidarity against hate‑driven extremism.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: An attack on a queer community space contrasts sharply with an episode that uplifts marginalized voices and asserts unity, resistance, and pride through hip‑hop.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2008, forensic tests confirmed that two sets of skeletal remains found near Yekaterinburg were those of Tsarevich Alexei and Grand Duchess Anastasia, children of Russia’s last Tsar. The discovery helped close a century‑long chapter of speculation, impostors, and myth surrounding the Romanov family’s fate.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The belated identification of Romanov remains is an act of historical closure and reflection, dovetailing with an episode about looking back, healing, and making peace with the past.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1961, while East–West tensions simmered, an entirely different kind of barrier was about to fall in future decades: in 2000s hindsight, the same continent would be knitted by the Web whose free protocols were announced on this date in 1993. From particle labs to bedrooms, Europe’s information borders began to dissolve into hyperlinks and shared screens.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The Web’s boundary‑blurring protocols mirror trip‑hop’s cross‑pollinated sound, both dissolving borders between places, genres, and ways of connecting.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1961, K‑19’s commissioning signaled a new underwater arms race; but decades later, humanity’s eyes and dreams turned upward again, into space. Each submarine patrol beneath the waves had its quiet analogue in satellites and probes drifting above our atmosphere, mapping the same planet from a different, silent ocean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

queue.match_reason: Submarines move through dark oceans while satellites sail the void; the episode’s 'space shift' metaphor resonates with reimagining emptiness, whether underwater or in orbit.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1963, the Bristol Bus Boycott began reshaping British civil rights; at the same time, popular music was quietly rewiring how people thought about race and solidarity. From ska and reggae to soul and hip‑hop, bus routes and soundwaves both became channels where integration and resistance could travel.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: The boycott’s challenge to racism in public transport resonates with Afrobeat’s politically charged rhythms, both insisting on freedom of movement and dignity.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1492, as Columbus’s commission promised him titles over lands he had never seen, other migrations and musical cultures were already crossing the Mediterranean silently. Long before 'discovery' narratives, the sea itself was a busy soundscape of oars, dialects, and songs flowing between Africa, Europe, and the Middle East.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: The commission that led to Atlantic voyages contrasts with the older, layered crossings of the Mediterranean that this episode celebrates through coastal music and shared waters.

published.fact_text: In 1897, at London’s Royal Institution, physicist J. J. Thomson announced that he had discovered a new kind of particle: the electron. By showing it was over 1,800 times lighter than a proton yet carried electric charge, he cracked open the idea of indivisible atoms and helped launch modern particle physics and electronics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A breakthrough in understanding the electron echoes the episode’s celebration of curiosity, science, and how learning reshapes our world.

published.fact_text: On April 30, 1905, Albert Einstein quietly submitted his doctoral thesis to the University of Zurich. This 'ordinary' academic milestone came in the same annus mirabilis in which he would reinvent space, time, and light, showing how life-changing ideas can emerge from a desk and a stack of equations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Einstein’s deep thinking and quiet focus contrast with today’s hyperconnected world, aligning with an episode about disconnection, reflection, and what happens when we think deeply.

published.fact_text: In 1939, NBC launched one of the world’s first regular television services in New York City by broadcasting Franklin D. Roosevelt’s opening address at the World’s Fair. Fewer than 1,000 people actually owned televisions to see it, making this historic moment feel more like a test transmission to the future than mass media.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: Both the NBC launch and Sedna FM’s first episode represent experimental first broadcasts, seeding new ways of experiencing sound and stories.

published.fact_text: On April 30, 1993, CERN announced that the protocols of the World Wide Web would be made available royalty‑free to anyone. That single decision turned a niche tool for physicists into a planetary nervous system, enabling everything from memes to online revolutions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: The Web’s free protocols shaped how we stream and discover music, while this episode questions that always‑on, infinite-stream reality and invites a more deliberate relationship with digital sound.

published.fact_text: In 1900, the Hawaiian Islands officially became a territory of the United States, with Sanford B. Dole as its first governor. This legal shift reshaped island identity, blending Indigenous Hawaiian culture with an intensifying wave of American political and economic influence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: The sun‑drenched, coastal imagery of Hawaii’s political transformation pairs with an episode built around seaside rhythms and an 'endless summer' atmosphere.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1979, Indonesia’s Mount Marapi erupted on the island of Sumatra, sending ash and gas into the sky and killing between 80 and 100 people. The volcano’s complex structure and frequent activity remind scientists how living near fertile volcanic soils comes with hidden, explosive risks.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the Marapi eruption and this episode orbit Earth’s raw forces — volcanoes and cold oceans — inviting awe for powerful, unpredictable natural phenomena.

published.fact_text: On April 30, 1492, the Spanish Crown formally commissioned Christopher Columbus as 'Admiral of the Ocean Sea' and governor of any lands he might find. This document turned one navigator’s risky idea into an officially sanctioned voyage that would disrupt entire continents and cultures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A royal commission that set ships across the Atlantic resonates with a seafaring, boat‑based episode about journeys over water and the stories they carry.

published.fact_text: In 1513, Edmund de la Pole, a Yorkist claimant to the English throne, was executed on the orders of Henry VIII. His beheading was both a brutal personal fate and a deliberate act to eliminate one of the last serious rivals to the Tudor dynasty.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: The ruthless power plays of Tudor courts fit the rebellious, confrontational spirit of rock, especially voices that refuse to submit to authority.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1598, Juan de Oñate began the conquest and colonization of Santa Fe de Nuevo México. His expedition forged one of the northernmost outposts of New Spain, leaving a legacy tangled with missionary work, Indigenous resistance, and contested memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A story of colonial expansion into Indigenous lands is mirrored by an episode centered on Tuareg voices, whose music carries histories of territory, resistance, and survival under outside powers.
